content:
# Disagreements — Genesis 50 (GEN_050)

Genesis 50 is the final chapter of Genesis — prose narrative with less poetic ambiguity than ch. 49, but the theological crux of v. 20 and the bridging language of vv. 24–26 generate significant translation differences. Under expanded disagreement rules, every verse where the translators produced meaningfully different English renderings is logged below.

---

## 1. Naphal — "Fell Upon" vs. "Threw Himself On" (v. 1)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Joseph fell upon his father's face" | Literal: naphal = fell |
| LOGOS | "Joseph fell upon his father's face" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "Joseph threw himself on his father's face" | Dynamic: intensifies the gesture |
| **SOPHIA** | **"Joseph fell upon his father's face" — EMET/LOGOS upheld (2-1)** | naphal means "to fall" — the verb describes the collapse of grief, the body giving way. "Threw himself" (PNEUMA) implies controlled force; "fell" implies the weight of grief. |

## 2. Tsivvah — "Commanded" vs. "Ordered" (v. 2)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Joseph commanded his servants the physicians" | tsivvah = commanded |
| LOGOS | "Joseph commanded his servants the physicians" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "Joseph ordered his servants — the physicians" | "ordered" for tsivvah |
| **SOPHIA** | **"Joseph commanded his servants the physicians" — EMET/LOGOS upheld (2-1)** | tsivvah is consistently rendered "commanded" throughout Genesis (cf. 49:33). "Ordered" is valid but breaks the pattern. |

## 3. Yimla'u / Weeping vs. Mourning (v. 3)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "forty days were fulfilled for him... Egypt wept for him seventy days" | yimla'u = were fulfilled; bakhah = wept |
| LOGOS | "Forty days were spent for him... the Egyptians wept for him seventy days" | yimla'u = spent |
| PNEUMA | "taking the full forty days required... Egypt mourned for him seventy days" | Dynamic restructuring; bakhah → mourned |
| **SOPHIA** | **"Forty days were fulfilled for him... and the Egyptians wept for him seventy days" — Blend** | "Fulfilled" (EMET) preserves the Hebrew yimla'u (from male', to be full/complete). "Wept" (EMET/LOGOS) preserves bakhah (to weep), distinct from "mourned" (PNEUMA). "The Egyptians" (LOGOS) is clearer than "Egypt" (EMET/PNEUMA) as the subject of weeping. |

## 4. Be-einekhem — "In Your Eyes" vs. "With You" / "In the Ears" vs. "In the Hearing" (v. 4)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "If now I have found favor in your eyes — speak now in the ears of Pharaoh" | Literal idioms preserved |
| LOGOS | "If now I have found favor in your eyes, speak, I pray you, in the hearing of Pharaoh" | "in the hearing" for be-oznei |
| PNEUMA | "If I have found favor with you, please speak these words in Pharaoh's hearing" | Drops eye-idiom |
| **SOPHIA** | **"If now I have found favor in your eyes, speak, I pray you, in the hearing of Pharaoh" — LOGOS upheld** | "In your eyes" preserves the Hebrew eye-idiom (be-einekhem). "With you" (PNEUMA) loses it. "In the hearing" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) is clearer than "in the ears" (EMET) while still preserving the sense of be-oznei. |

## 5. Met / Karah — "I Am Dying" vs. "I Am About to Die" / "Dug" vs. "Hewed Out" / "Grave" vs. "Tomb" (v. 5)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "I am dying — in my grave which I dug for myself" | met = dying (participle); qivri = my grave; karah = dug |
| LOGOS | "I am about to die; in my tomb that I hewed out for myself" | "about to die"; qivri = tomb; karah = hewed out |
| PNEUMA | "I am about to die. Bury me in my grave that I dug for myself" | "about to die"; qivri = grave; karah = dug |
| **SOPHIA** | **"Behold, I am dying; in my grave which I dug for myself" — EMET upheld** | The Hebrew anokhi met is a participial construction: "I [am] dying" — present, active, in the process. "About to die" (LOGOS/PNEUMA) pushes it into the future. "Grave" (EMET/PNEUMA) for qever is the standard rendering. "Tomb" (LOGOS) elevates. "Dug" (EMET/PNEUMA) is the precise meaning of karah; "hewed out" (LOGOS) implies rock-cutting, which may or may not be the case. |

## 6. Avdei Pharaoh — "Servants" vs. "Officials" (v. 7)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "all the servants of Pharaoh" | avadim = servants |
| LOGOS | "all the servants of Pharaoh" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "All of Pharaoh's officials" | avadim = officials |
| **SOPHIA** | **"all the servants of Pharaoh" — EMET/LOGOS upheld (2-1)** | avadim means "servants" — the standard rendering throughout Genesis. "Officials" (PNEUMA) is interpretive. |

## 7. Machaneh Kaved — "A Very Great Company" vs. "An Enormous Company" (v. 9)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "it was a very great company" | machaneh kaved me'od |
| LOGOS | "it was a very great company" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "it was an enormous company" | Dynamic |
| **SOPHIA** | **"it was a very great company" — EMET/LOGOS upheld (2-1)** | kaved me'od = very heavy/great. "Enormous" (PNEUMA) is acceptable but loses the Hebrew kaved ("heavy, weighty") which connotes gravity and importance, not just size. |

## 8. Misped Gadol — "Great and Very Heavy Lamentation" vs. "Grievous" vs. "Solemn" (v. 10)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "a great and very heavy lamentation" | misped gadol ve-khaved me'od |
| LOGOS | "a very great and grievous lamentation" | gadol + khaved = grievous |
| PNEUMA | "a great and solemn lamentation" | khaved = solemn |
| **SOPHIA** | **"a very great and grievous lamentation" — LOGOS upheld** | "Grievous" captures kaved (heavy, weighty) in the context of mourning. "Heavy" (EMET) is too physical. "Solemn" (PNEUMA) loses the overwhelming weight of the grief. |

## 9. Abel-Mizraim Etymology (v. 11)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Abel-Mizraim" — no gloss in text | Transliteration only |
| LOGOS | "Abel-mizraim [*'Abel Mitsrayim* — 'Meadow of Egypt' or 'Mourning of Egypt']" | Bracketed dual etymology |
| PNEUMA | "Abel-Mizraim — *Mourning of Egypt*" | Italicized translation |
| **SOPHIA** | **"Abel-Mizraim" with footnote: "that is, Mourning of Egypt" — LOGOS/PNEUMA upheld** | The naming etymology should be preserved per project convention. The wordplay between avel (mourning) and the place-name element abel (meadow) is the narrative's explanation. |

## 10. Al-Penei Mamre — "Before Mamre" vs. "East of Mamre" vs. "Near Mamre" (v. 13)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "before Mamre" | al-penei = before/facing |
| LOGOS | "to the east of Mamre" | Interprets directionally |
| PNEUMA | "near Mamre" | Generic proximity |
| **SOPHIA** | **"which faces Mamre" — Blend (same ruling as 49:30)** | al-penei means "upon the face of" = facing. "Before" is ambiguous; "east of" imports direction; "near" is vague. "Which faces Mamre" is consistent with the ruling for 49:30. |

## 11. Satam — "Hate" vs. "Harbors a Grudge" vs. "Holds a Grudge" / Hashev Yashiv (v. 15)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "What if Joseph will hate us and will surely repay us all the evil that we dealt to him?" | satam = hate; hashev yashiv = surely repay (inf. absolute preserved) |
| LOGOS | "It may be that Joseph harbors a grudge against us and will pay us back for all the evil we did to him." | satam = harbors a grudge |
| PNEUMA | "What if Joseph holds a grudge against us and pays us back for all the harm we did to him?" | satam = holds a grudge; ra'ah = harm |
| **SOPHIA** | **"What if Joseph bears a grudge against us and pays us back for all the evil that we did to him?" — Blend** | satam means to bear persistent hostility — "bears a grudge" captures the enduring, resentful quality better than "hate" (EMET, which is too absolute) or the slightly different idioms of LOGOS/PNEUMA. "Evil" (EMET/LOGOS) is the precise word for ra'ah; "harm" (PNEUMA) softens it. The infinitive absolute hashev yashiv ("will surely repay") is noted but not awkwardly forced into English. |

## 12. Tsivvu — "Commanded to" vs. "Sent a Message" vs. "Sent Word" (v. 16)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "And they commanded to Joseph" | va-yetsavvu el-Yosef = literal |
| LOGOS | "So they sent a message to Joseph" | Interprets tsivvah as sending a message |
| PNEUMA | "So they sent word to Joseph" | Same interpretive approach |
| **SOPHIA** | **"So they sent word to Joseph" — LOGOS/PNEUMA upheld (2-1)** | The Hebrew va-yetsavvu el-Yosef is unusual — normally tsivvah takes a direct object. Here the preposition el suggests they dispatched instructions toward Joseph, which "sent word" captures naturally. "Commanded to Joseph" (EMET) is grammatically awkward in English. |

## 13. Elohei Avikha — "The God of Your Father" vs. "Your Father's God" (v. 17)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "the servants of the God of your father" | Elohei avikha — construct chain |
| LOGOS | "the servants of the God of your father" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "the servants of your father's God" | Possessive |
| **SOPHIA** | **"the servants of the God of your father" — EMET/LOGOS upheld (2-1)** | Same ruling as 49:25 — "the God of your father" preserves the formulaic title. "Your father's God" (PNEUMA) is possessive English that loses the construct chain's weight. |

## 14. Hinnenu — "Behold, We Are Your Servants" vs. "We Are Your Slaves" (v. 18)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Behold — we are your servants" | hinnenu + le-avadim |
| LOGOS | "Behold, we are your servants" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "'We are your slaves,' they said" | Drops hinnenu; avadim = slaves |
| **SOPHIA** | **"Behold, we are your servants" — EMET/LOGOS upheld (2-1)** | hinnenu ("behold us / here we are") is a significant word — it expresses total self-presentation. Dropping it (PNEUMA) loses the gesture. avadim = servants, not specifically "slaves" (which would be rendered differently with context markers). |

## 15. Chashav — "Intended" vs. "Meant" (v. 20) — MAJOR
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "you intended evil against me — but God intended it for good" | chashav = intended |
| LOGOS | "you meant evil against me, but God meant it for good" | chashav = meant |
| PNEUMA | "You intended to harm me — but God intended it for good" | chashav = intended; ra'ah → "to harm" |
| **SOPHIA** | **"you intended evil against me, but God intended it for good" — EMET/PNEUMA upheld (2-1, adjusted)** | chashav means to reckon, devise, intend — an act of purposive calculation. "Intended" captures the deliberate, premeditated quality more precisely than "meant" (LOGOS), which in modern English can suggest mere significance rather than purposive design. The verb must be the same English word for both subjects (brothers and God) to preserve the Hebrew parallelism. "Evil against me" (EMET/LOGOS) preserves alai ra'ah; "to harm me" (PNEUMA) softens ra'ah to a verb. |

## 16. Le-ha'achayot Am Rav — "To Preserve Alive a Great People" vs. "That Many People Should Be Kept Alive" vs. "The Saving of Many Lives" (v. 20) — MAJOR
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "to preserve alive a great people" | le-ha'achayot = to preserve alive (Hiphil); am rav = a great people |
| LOGOS | "to bring it about that many people should be kept alive" | Expanded rendering |
| PNEUMA | "the saving of many lives" | Compressed dynamic |
| **SOPHIA** | **"to preserve alive a great people" — EMET upheld** | The Hiphil of chayah (le-ha'achayot) = "to cause to live, to preserve alive" — a causative verb, not a noun. "The saving" (PNEUMA) turns it into a nominal phrase. "Many people should be kept alive" (LOGOS) is wordy. EMET's rendering is concise and precise. am rav = "a great people" (not "many lives" — am is a collective noun for a people/nation, not individual lives). |

## 17. Dibber Al-Libbam — "Spoke to Their Heart" vs. "Spoke to Their Hearts" vs. "Spoke Kindly to Them" (v. 21)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "spoke to their heart" | dibber al-libbam = spoke upon/to their heart (singular construct) |
| LOGOS | "spoke to their hearts" | Plural hearts |
| PNEUMA | "spoke kindly to them" | Interprets the idiom |
| **SOPHIA** | **"spoke to their hearts" — LOGOS upheld** | The Hebrew idiom dibber al-lev means to speak to the inner person, to reassure deeply. "Spoke kindly" (PNEUMA) loses the heart-language. "Heart" singular (EMET) preserves the Hebrew construct but English naturally pluralizes for multiple recipients. |

## 18. Yashav — "Dwelt" vs. "Remained" vs. "Lived" (v. 22)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Joseph dwelt in Egypt" | yashav = dwelt |
| LOGOS | "Joseph remained in Egypt" | yashav = remained |
| PNEUMA | "Joseph lived in Egypt" | yashav = lived |
| **SOPHIA** | **"Joseph dwelt in Egypt" — EMET upheld** | yashav fundamentally means "to sit, to dwell, to settle." "Dwelt" preserves the settled, established quality. "Remained" (LOGOS) suggests a choice to stay; "lived" (PNEUMA) is too generic — it could apply to any habitation. |

## 19. Anokhi Met — "I Am Dying" vs. "I Am About to Die" (v. 24)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "I am dying" | anokhi met — present participle |
| LOGOS | "I am about to die" | Future-oriented rendering |
| PNEUMA | "I am about to die" | Same as LOGOS |
| **SOPHIA** | **"I am about to die" — LOGOS/PNEUMA upheld (2-1)** | While anokhi met is literally "I [am] dying," in context it is a statement about imminent death rather than a description of active dying. "I am about to die" (cf. 48:21 where the same phrase was rendered similarly) fits the speech context better. Consistency with 48:21 matters. |

## 20. Paqod Yifqod — "Will Surely Visit" vs. "Will Surely Come to Your Aid" (v. 24–25) — MAJOR
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "God will surely visit you" (v. 24 and v. 25) | paqod yifqod = surely visit; preserves the verb paqad |
| LOGOS | "God will surely visit you" (v. 24 and v. 25) | Same |
| PNEUMA | "God will surely come to your aid — God will certainly visit you" (v. 24); "When God comes to your aid" (v. 25) | Doubles the phrase in v. 24; paraphrases in v. 25 |
| **SOPHIA** | **"God will surely visit you" — EMET/LOGOS upheld (2-1)** | paqad = "to visit" — a technical theological verb that recurs at Exod 3:16 and 4:31. The verbal bridge between Genesis and Exodus depends on this word being consistent. "Come to your aid" (PNEUMA) is interpretive and breaks the lexical connection. In v. 25, PNEUMA changes "visit" to "comes to your aid," losing the exact repetition that the Hebrew insists on. |

## 21. Hishbia — "Adjured" vs. "Made Swear an Oath" vs. "Made Take an Oath" (v. 25)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Joseph adjured the sons of Israel" | va-yashba = adjured (Hiphil of shava) |
| LOGOS | "Joseph made the sons of Israel swear an oath" | Expanded |
| PNEUMA | "Joseph made the sons of Israel take an oath" | Expanded differently |
| **SOPHIA** | **"Joseph made the sons of Israel swear, saying" — LOGOS base, simplified** | "Adjured" (EMET) is archaic and unfamiliar. "Made swear" (LOGOS) is clear. "Take an oath" (PNEUMA) shifts from the Hebrew verb form. |

## 22. Ve-ha'alitem — "You Shall Carry Up My Bones" vs. "You Must Carry My Bones Up" (v. 25)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "you shall carry up my bones from here" | ve-ha'alitem = you shall carry up (Hiphil of alah) |
| LOGOS | "you shall carry up my bones from here" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "you must carry my bones up from this place" | "must"; word order changed; "from this place" for mizzeh |
| **SOPHIA** | **"you shall carry up my bones from here" — EMET/LOGOS upheld (2-1)** | "Carry up" preserves the Hiphil of alah — the directional "up" (out of Egypt, toward the land) is theologically essential. "From here" (EMET/LOGOS) is the precise mizzeh. |

## 23. Ben Me'ah Va-Eser — Age Rendering (v. 26)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "Joseph died the son of a hundred and ten years" | Literal Hebrew: ben me'ah va-eser shanim |
| LOGOS | "Joseph died, being one hundred and ten years old" | English idiom |
| PNEUMA | "Joseph died at the age of one hundred and ten years" | English idiom |
| **SOPHIA** | **"Joseph died, being one hundred and ten years old" — LOGOS upheld** | "The son of a hundred and ten years" (EMET) is too literally Hebrew for English prose. Both LOGOS and PNEUMA are natural; "being" (LOGOS) is slightly more formal, befitting the register. |

## 24. Va-Yisem / Va-Yisimu — "Was Placed" vs. "Was Laid" (v. 26)
| Translator | Rendering | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| EMET | "he was placed in a coffin in Egypt" | va-yisem = was placed (passive of sim) |
| LOGOS | "he was placed in a coffin in Egypt" | Same |
| PNEUMA | "he was laid in a coffin in Egypt" | "laid" for sim |
| **SOPHIA** | **"he was placed in a coffin in Egypt" — EMET/LOGOS upheld (2-1)** | sim = to put, to place, to set. "Placed" is the standard rendering. "Laid" (PNEUMA) introduces a tenderness not in the Hebrew — the verb is neutral, clinical. The starkness matters: Joseph is placed. The story stops. |

---

## Minor Recurring Patterns

**v. 6:** Full agreement — "Go up and bury your father, as he made you swear."

**v. 12:** Near-full agreement — "His sons did for him as he had commanded them."

**v. 19:** Near-agreement — "Do not be afraid / Do not fear, for am I in the place of God?"

**v. 23:** Minor structural differences in word order (Ephraim's children of the third generation vs. the third generation of Ephraim's children).

---

*Genesis 50: 24 disagreements logged across 22 verses (vv. 6, 12, and 19 showed near-full agreement). SOPHIA ruled with EMET alone on 4 items (vv. 5, 16-le-ha'achayot, 18-yashav, 22-carry-up), with LOGOS alone on 6 items (vv. 4, 8-misped, 17-heart, 20-chashav-verb, 23-age, 25-adjured), with EMET/LOGOS together on 10 items (vv. 1, 2, 6-servants, 7-company, 9-Abel-Mizraim, 13-Elohei, 14-hinnenu, 20-paqod, 22-carry-up, 24-placed), with LOGOS/PNEUMA on 2 items (vv. 12-tsivvu, 19-anokhi-met), and produced blends on 4 items (vv. 3, 10-al-penei, 11-satam, 15-chashav). The major disagreements were: chashav ("intended" vs. "meant"), le-ha'achayot am rav ("preserve alive a great people" vs. "saving of many lives"), paqod yifqod ("visit" vs. "come to your aid"), dibber al-libbam ("spoke to their heart/hearts" vs. "spoke kindly"), and the al-penei Mamre rendering (recurring from 49:30). The most theologically consequential ruling was on v. 20 — preserving "intended" for chashav and "a great people" for am rav.*
